data.title: ICAP: Compelling Actively Managed Equity Strategy Facilitating Megatrend Exposure
data.description:
The Infrastructure Capital Equity Income ETF (ICAP) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to maximize income and total returns by investing in dividend-paying equities and equity-like securities across all market capitalizations. To enhance growth and income, ICAP also writes call and put options, in part to hedge risk, enter or exit positions, and increase exposure. With its diversified approach to the markets, ICAP has the potential to provide alpha through dynamic positioning, an appealing approach that is oftentimes reserved for closed-end funds. Given its strong historical performance and transparent positioning, I recommend ICAP with a Buy rating.

Investment Thesis for ICAP

ICAP takes the broadest approach to creating its coverage universe, including everything from common stock, preferred shares, convertible notes, as well as options derivatives to create diversified exposure for investors. While the fund primarily invests in domestic securities, it does have the flexibility to regionally diversify through the use of ADRs, GDRs, and IDRs. In addition to this, the fund isn't limited to common stock, providing bandwidth to invest in real estate investment trusts [REITs] and master limited partnerships [MLPs], allowing the fund to diversify its investable universe into markets that may be less accessible to some investors and brokerage accounts due to tax reporting [K-1]. Accordingly, ICAP will issue a 1099 for tax reporting purposes, allowing for the fund to be utilized in tax-advantaged accounts.

The fund takes a hybrid approach to constructing the portfolio, starting at the top-down, top of the funnel with a macroeconomic view before building a bottom-up case for each individual security. For example, the top 3 holdings within the strategy include Marvell Technology (MRVL), Amazon (AMZN), and Citizens Financial Group (CFG).

Marvell Technology is one of the leading custom silicon and network silicon developers next to Broadcom (AVGO) in a market that is gradually turning towards XPUs for AI inferencing. Marvell recently announced that it is in discussions with Alphabet (GOOG) to develop two new chip designs, a memory processing unit and a new tensor processing unit. An agreement with Alphabet could deliver substantial growth for Marvell over the coming years as data center investments are expected to continue to expand to facilitate broader adoptions for enterprise and physical AI. Accordingly, more than $4T is expected to be invested towards computing hardware by 2030, a major upside potential for Marvell to participate in. MRVL shares account for 5.78% of the total portfolio weight.

Amazon is one of the leading hyperscalers developing gigawatts of data center capacity to drive growth for its cloud services customers. In a recent report covering the AMZN shares, I outlined the acquisition of Globalstar (GSAT) and how the satellite network may enhance and accelerate Amazon's internal physical AI adoption given Globalstar's 2-way data connection technology for industrial IoT. My theory is that Amazon may use this infrastructure to actively manage and monitor fully autonomous warehouses as well as final-mile delivery services in the coming years as AI-enabled robotics become more widely adopted. AMZN shares account for 5.27% of the total portfolio weight.

From a top-down perspective, ICAP presently favors electric utilities (8.39%), semiconductors (7.42%), and homebuilding (7%) as its top sector allocations. Some of the top holdings that fall into these categories include NextEra Energy (NEE) at 3.76%, Lennar Corporation (LEN) at 3.41%, and NVIDIA (NVDA) at 2.64%.

I believe that the fund is taking an appealing approach to the market given the growing demand for electricity driven by reindustrialization and data center developments. Utilities are expected to invest anywhere from $1.1T to $1.4T over the next 5 years to harden the grid, modernize infrastructure, and expand capacity. For reference, this is roughly equivalent to the level of investments over the last decade. While capital deployed will in part fall on the utilities, the growth aspect of the investments can deliver stronger cash flows in the coming years supported by market demand for electricity, potentially providing greater shareholder value and growth as opposed to maintaining the historical defensive label.

Overall, I believe the dynamic approach to constructing the strategy can be appealing to investors seeking a managed strategy without utilizing the CEF wrapper. The benefit to investors is that the fund is fully transparent in its holdings, allowing investors to understand what they own when buying shares of the ETF. With the convergence of sector rotation and fundamental security selection, I believe ICAP can continue to grow exceptionally well and deliver alpha to investors.

One factor that investors must consider with the fund is its options trading strategy. The fund will primarily sell call and put options to increase exposure to the market as well as to enter and exit positions. This can be an appealing component that can deliver income for the portfolio given the premium received from writing options derivatives. The downside risk is that the covered calls may limit the full upside potential of the equity securities within the strategy, potentially creating some drag on NAV. In addition to this, the fund may be exposed to certain risks regarding its written put options and be forced to purchase shares of a company if the stock were to undergo significant volatility. While the fund has performed exceptionally well when compared to the S&P 500 (SPX) and the Nasdaq-100 (NDX), these factors could create some headwinds during volatile trading periods.

About Infrastructure Capital Equity Income ETF

ICAP was launched by Infrastructure Capital Advisors on December 28, 2021, on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy has a management fee of 80bps and a total expense ratio of 164bps. ICAP exhibits relatively thin liquidity with roughly $100mm in net assets, with an average of $753k in share value changing hands on a daily basis. As a result, ICAP has a relatively wide 30-day average bid/ask spread of 0.35%, potentially adding some costs when entering and exiting a position. Given that the strategy is actively managed, investors considering this fund will benefit from taking a buy-and-hold approach rather than actively trading the fund.

ICAP pays out a monthly distribution, which has exhibited growth to its regular monthly rate since inception. The fund paid out $2.62/share over the last twelve months for a yield of 9.10%.

Risks Related to ICAP

ICAP is an actively managed ETF designed to optimize total returns, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. ICAP is actively managed, exposing investors to manager risk and the portfolio management team's ability to appropriately select sectors, securities, and security weights. The strategy also employs options derivatives, which in many cases are covered calls with some naked calls, as well as short put options to gain broader exposure to the market. Covered calls may cap performance to a certain extent, while naked call options may increase risk. In some cases, options derivatives are utilized to enter and exit positions, meaning that the covered calls and short put options are strategically utilized to gain exposure to a stock that the portfolio management team may have had the intent to purchase. Thin liquidity may add additional costs to the total ownership of the fund, though the fund should be considered for a buy-and-hold strategy rather than for active trading.

Final Thoughts

ICAP can provide investors with an appealing dynamic approach to investing in the broader market in an ETF wrapper. With active sector allocation and security selection, I believe ICAP is set up to generate returns for shareholders, particularly when considering the current exposure. I am recommending ICAP with a Buy rating for long-term investors.

data.title: Compass Minerals: Pittsburgh's Ice Reveals The Value Chain
data.description:
After this winter, we are likely to see pieces moving on the chessboard following the recent news surrounding Compass Minerals (CMP). To begin with, last January the company had accumulated a one-month delay in salt deliveries to around 100 communities in southwestern Pennsylvania, grouped under SHACOG.

In fact, the executive director of this consortium stated that the company did not seem prepared for a winter in western Pennsylvania. Perhaps those words did not sit well, and in a market operating with depleted inventories and demand through the roof, it was not worth renewing the contract. Or perhaps there was indeed an operational failure, and CMP has decided to use the opportunity to seek an exit.

What is clear is that the Great Lakes were partially frozen, with Lake Erie nearly 96% frozen in January, disrupting part of the salt shipments. And in a city like Cleveland, which entered that month with reserves below 10,000 tons, a single storm would have consumed around 4,000 tons or even 7,000 tons.

So now it is time to understand how CMP operates within the value chain and whether a situation like last winter could happen again.

An Abundant Mineral With A Tight Market

It seems ironic that there can be a shortage of one of the most abundant minerals in the world. With underground deposits and the oceans alone, salt reserves are practically inexhaustible, so the answer ends up being simple and obvious. There will be as much salt as long as the cost of extracting it and transporting it leaves profits for producers. Like everything else in economics.

Although the global market is huge, buyers usually seek the nearest producers, so it ends up being a regional market. For example, the price of rock salt used on roads is around $56 per ton at plants in the United States. From there, transportation alone can account for nearly 50% of the total cost.

Of this salt, approximately 41% in the United States goes to road deicing, while 39% goes to the chemical industry, since salt is necessary to produce chlorine and caustic soda. This last point is important to highlight because without this salt, chlorine cannot be produced, and without chlorine, PVC, disinfectants, or aluminum cannot be produced. Alternatives are still too expensive to remove salt from the equation.

What do I mean by all this? A company like CMP stands on the front line to keep these industries operating due to its business model. Further back in the chain, there is nothing left but geology. And because of its geographic position, CMP’s Goderich Mine in Ontario can reach markets such as Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, Pittsburgh, Buffalo, Milwaukee, Minneapolis, Cincinnati, St. Louis, Toronto, and Montreal through the Great Lakes, with an advantage of roughly $5 to $10 per ton versus competitors relying on truck or rail.

And if we also consider that this product trades between $56 and $74 per ton, that advantage represents between 7% and 18% structurally. So I count two competitive advantages: first, a mine with a capacity of 9 million tons annually and reserves for 120 years; second, and more importantly, access to major end markets.

Position Of Power, Operational Failure, Or Both At The Same Time

So, going back to my initial question, was the SHACOG situation a natural event that was difficult to offset, an operational failure, or a position of power by Compass Minerals? To answer that question, I am going to lay out all the pieces of the puzzle in an attempt to reach a conclusion, just as I promised.

I begin with 2025, when Cargill sold all of its mines and exited the rock salt market. In the end, when one of the major players leaves the map, contracts are rising, as is the case in western New York by 35%, and logistical capacity is difficult to replicate, if not impossible. You have a scenario of power from the supply side if the new conditions are not attractive when the market is in your favor.

So was it an operational failure? For CMP, it was the first year in the Pittsburgh region. The salt leaves its mines and travels by barge to the RiverLift Industries depot in West Elizabeth for later truck distribution. At this point, it is unclear whether it was due to inventory or conditions, but starting on January 14, delays began, and in cities such as Scott Township, Castle Shannon, Kennedy Township, and Dormont, critical levels started to be reported.

Another cause is that the Allegheny River, Monongahela River, and Ohio River had between 3 and 6 inches of ice, which blocked barge traffic. So SHACOG pointed out that the company’s problem was failing to stock adequately to meet Pittsburgh demand before the freeze began, which later resulted in shipping disruptions.

Could it have been avoided then? I believe both sides are right, with the added factor that it is harder to assign blame when extreme events occur that, wrongly, are usually not planned for because they are considered less probable. In other words, according to SHACOG documentation, CMP did not meet the agreed supply before the rivers froze. But on the other hand, having Pittsburgh’s three rivers freeze simultaneously, while Lake Erie reached 96%, makes proper planning more difficult in my view.

How Much Does Losing SHACOG Really Hurt?

For this article, we have enough material to answer the question of what impact the SHACOG news will have. Pennsylvania consumes roughly 844,000 tons of road salt per year, and SHACOG groups around 100 municipalities in the southwest that represent between 8% and 12% of state consumption, meaning around 85,000 tons by my estimate.

CMP has guided for FY26 highway deicing volumes between 820,000 and 850,000 tons, so those 85,000 tons represent approximately 1% of the total. Therefore, I think the revenue impact is not as material as it may seem. With pricing at $73.96 per ton this past Q1 FY26, the loss equals around $6.3 million against total revenue of $1.3 billion.

I also believe this situation is already reflected in company guidance, since that outlook was issued in February, and the strategy management declared is to prioritize value over volume. That fits with losing SHACOG.

Moreover, my opinion is that if the needle moves, it looks more likely to move upward. If the 26-27 bid season materializes, for example, a 5% price increase would translate into roughly $12 million of additional EBITDA on its contract base, more than what it loses with SHACOG. So with the western New York contracts already mentioned, the Indiana RPF closing in May, and management commenting on low industry inventories, I see a structurally favorable setup for CMP.

In fact, results from the past Q1 showed sales growing 29% YoY to $396 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $65.3 million rising +103% YoY, and positive net income since 2023. So if we consider the stock price, trading around 8.5x forward EV/EBITDA, and although its average is higher than comparables, I also see a situation that justifies that premium, with the debt factor acting as a penalty. Otherwise, we would likely see an even higher multiple.

My Reading On CMP At These Prices

Could the same thing happen again this winter? Regarding salt, my opinion is yes. In other words, a mild winter could crush volumes as in previous years, while a harsher winter would send demand surging again.

But structurally, CMP’s position has changed considering that Cargill is out of the market. And if we add depleted inventories, while the first contracts of the bid season are already showing increases, I see the company in a stronger position.

In the next article on Compass Minerals, I will discuss its second business in greater depth, as it is also relevant, of course. The sulfate of potash produced from the Great Salt Lake, which is used by farmers in California for almond, strawberry, and pistachio production, has gone from operating losses of around $86 million in FY24 to positive FY26 guidance of $34 million.

I believe CMP is experiencing a business recovery, something also confirmed by the trend in the stock price. I think this is a favorable enough scenario for that trend to continue, with a better business than in the last three years, assets that are geographically irreplaceable, and a market backdrop that I believe is the most favorable in years.

data.title: Halvio Capital Q1 2026 Letter To Partners
data.description:
Dear Partners,

Accounts managed by Halvio were up 14.01% for Q1 2026 compared to the major indices that were either roughly flat to down slightly.

Halvio S&P Dow TSX Russell 2000
FY 2025* 24.94% 21.40% 14.34% 25.13% 23.10%
Q1 2026 14.01% -4.33% -0.90% 3.94% 0.90%

Large winners for the quarter included Cipher (CPHR), Mestek (MCCK), QEP (QEPI) and Beng Kuang Marine.

Cipher’s product Natroba has started to show some growth and the balance sheet is now back in pristine shape. An increase in the stock price gives the management team another method of potential acquisition currency rather than just cash or debt financing. Mestek and QEP are very illiquid OTC stocks that don’t need much volume to move the price up or down. Both trade for cheap EV/EBIT multiples compared to peers and have substantial net cash balance sheets. Mestek just released its 2025 annual report and grew sales in its metal forming and HVAC segments for the year and continues to remain extremely profitable.

Beng Kuang purchased the remaining 49% of their subsidiary ASOM for S$60m using a mix of stock, cash and contingent consideration. This subsidiary has been their crown jewel and what has delivered a lot of the value to shareholders over the past few years. The company put out a recent presentation that highlights some key aspects of their business. While their fortunes are tied to the price of oil, they are able to capture two different growth avenues: servicing aging FPSO fleets AND potential expansion of new FPSOs coming to the market.

I feel pretty fortunate that we didn’t have any type of exposure to SAAS/tech as most of that sector has sold off due to fears of AI wiping out their business models. It has never appealed to me to pay such high multiples for companies in industries that can change in the blink of an eye, with little hard asset value but only assets comprising the software and other intangibles that could be made worthless by someone with an idea and a computer. Another market worry was the war in the Middle East that has caused oil prices to surge over 50%. While the situation remains ongoing, the long term ramifications to the global markets remains highly uncertain and I have no idea what the end result will look like.

Starting last fall I decided to look at the US OTC market and wrote a blog post about it here.

During the quarter I became interested in a few companies based in Singapore and have taken a position in one on top of our current BEZ position. I also took a position in a tiny Canadian newspaper company detailed below. The small cap space continues to produce a ton of ideas and I currently have more ideas than capital.

Sincerely,

Anthony

New Idea: FP Newspapers Inc (FP.V)

FP Newspapers (FP) is a tiny $5m microcap company that flies under the radar. FP owns 49% of a partnership that owns the leading newspaper in Canada's 6th largest town, Winnipeg, called the Winnipeg Free Press and some other smaller news outlets. FP was formerly an income tax trust for Canadian tax purposes that owned securities issued by a limited partnership that itself owned a few Canadian newspapers. Due to the implementation of the SIFT tax in the early 2010s in Canada, the trust ownership structure lost its tax advantage and FP then converted into a corporation to own the limited partnership and went off the TSX on to the smaller TSX Venture Exchange. This current structure is in place today and shareholders of FP own 49% of the limited partnership.

Newspapers make most of their money from advertising and circulation (actual sale of the paper). The rise of the internet and digital advertising has resulted in attention and advertising dollars moving online and away from papers, which has resulted in the industry declining over the past few decades. You can see the steady decline in FP's total revenues over the last few years:

2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
Total revenues 67 64 53 55 57 53 49
YoY% -4.48% -17.19% 3.77% 3.64% -7.02% -7.55%

However, the Winnipeg Free Press is extremely embedded in the 803,000 residents of Winnipeg with 46% of Winnipeg adults reading the Free Press on a weekly basis. And the breakdown of the revenue numbers tell that. While print advertising has declined most years, circulation revenue has remained extremely resilient.

2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
print advertising 36.7 33.17 24.4 23.6 24.1 20.6 18.3
% of rev. 55% 52% 46% 43% 42% 39% 37%
circulation 24.3 24.5 24.1 24.8 24.1 23.4 23.1
% of rev. 36% 38% 45% 45% 42% 44% 47%
Other 5.96 6.13 4.861 6.2 8.5 8.7 7.2
Total revenues 68 65 54 55 58 54 49

Even with the decline in overall revenues, FP manages to maintain a reliable $6m-$7m of EBITDA per year. In 2022 management decided to invest in a printing press that they would move from New Jersey to Winnipeg for $10m using debt financing, only to abandon the project in 2023. Since that time revenue has continued to decrease but the free cash flow of the business has remained flat and has been used to pay off the debt.

With a stock price of $0.70 and shares outstanding of 6.9m, FP has a $4.8m market cap. The underlying partnership has net cash of 2m after having paid down all its debt and just did $6m EBITDA for 2025. Without taking account for the net cash, the look through EV/EBITDA is 2x . FP owns the building it operates in and based on conservative property tax records could be worth $22m. Taking FP's 49% share of that would value the company double its current market cap. This hidden asset provides significant downside protection and there is a potential catalyst on the horizon of management putting some of that cash build up to work for shareholders.

Believe it or not there are still buyers for these types of assets. Just last year the DallaNews Corporation (DALN) decided to sell its building and then right after there was a bidding war for the operating business. A logical acquiror of FP in my eyes would be Torstar Corporation, which owns a collection of Canadian newspapers in some mid-sized Canadian towns. While I don't think the business needs to be sold to do well from here, it does show there is demand for these declining niche assets.

FP is cheap today for a few reasons:

  • They use the equity accounting method for its main asset which makes it hard to screen for as a quick glance or view of the financial statements won't tell the whole story.
  • The building FP operates out of is measured at its historical cost
  • $5m market cap makes it too small and illiquid for most funds to own
  • It doesn't own the underlying newspaper asset 100% outright but just shy of control at 49%
  • Buying into a declining newspaper company is not very enticing to most investors
  • Questionable past capital allocation that resulted in a large one-time write off of $8m right after deciding to make a significant investment in a printing press

References

  1. Accounts managed started April 1, 2025.

Disclosure: None

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Carl Zeiss Meditec: Hitting The Bottom
data.description:
Carl Zeiss Meditec (CZMWF) (CZMWY) is a company that I have been reviewing for some 1-2 years at this point, with a stake (investment) in my portfolio, and one that I am planning to expand. The reasoning behind my decision is simple - and in this article, I'll update it for the sub-€30/share price.

Investing with a value orientation or perspective is hard. It's especially hard when you believe you've "caught" the company at a good price relative to where it has been previously, but it turns out that the price you got was only the precursor to a further decline. That's what has happened here. In about 2.5 years, the company has erased almost 10 years of premiumization from the planet and from the share price. The last time Carl Zeiss Meditec traded at something like a 15-18x normalized P/E was way back after the GFC in 2010.

This can mean one of two things. Either the company is incredibly undervalued, and the market is making a massive mistake based on an incorrect interpretation of earnings signals, or the nature of the "dip" the company is going through. This is one option. If you invest, you probably know that the market has a strong tendency to overreact on both the short and the long side. Companies go up way more than they should in the short term, and down way more than they should, again in the short term. The problem usually is that you don't know whether you're in "short-term-land" until it's too late, and the valuation, one way or another, has moved in a way that precludes you from taking profitable advantage of the situation. It requires you to recognize what happens in advance, one way or another. That is something that I try to do using fundamental metrics and my conservative approach.

The second option is, obviously, that the company is experiencing a structural decline in its business. When I say structural, that's also what I mean. It implies collapse. It implies that it will not recover. It implies that the movement is a justified directional movement due to factual, underlying business trends that are not expected in any realistic scenario to recover.

You probably already know what my stance is given the circumstances as well as this piece, but let me lay out my case for you and why I believe this is the very definition of an overreaction.

Because if the company isn't facing a structural decline, that means you have to at least admit to the possibility of this reaction being to some degree unjustified in the context of the longer-term trends. You can find my last article on the company here.

Carl Zeiss Meditec - A bottom is hard to determine in the short term

Finding the bottom for a company trading in the negative is very tricky, especially when the company has an established history of over a decade of trading at a massive premium, as this company has.

This is hardly the first or the only time I'm calling for the company to be rated a "BUY" or even a "STRONG BUY" - or even the third. I have been positive on this company for some time.

The company has now experienced a 60% share price decline. It's moved from well over €70/share for the native to below €30/share. Why? Because, as I've said in the past, a convergence of challenges. The primary challenge that I would say the company has been facing is the company's Chinese market trends.

It's this Chinese market challenge that was directly responsible for the decline in 1Q, which was reported not long ago. The company's revenue declined 2%, but the EBITDA crashed, with a margin below 2%. Obviously, not a favorable development in any way.

So what happened?

In my previous article, I spoke to risks relating to the company's price/mix, which is exactly what happened here. In the main segment, the company saw issues in its refractive phasing business and some sales losses in China. The Microsurgery segment suffered FX as well as an unfavorable product mix.

From what I can read from the filings and management comments, aside from China, it's mostly FX and product mix. To this comes something like tariffs as well (which increases NA competition given the pricing increases this results in), which is expressed in a 12.7% decline in sales.

A company like Zeiss, in a situation like this, faces the unfortunate combination of high fixed costs (resulting in what we can call a negative operating leverage), which means that even with declining or low revenues, the same sort of fixed costs remain.

So, how to turn this around?

First off, many of the effects seen here were non-recurring. I doubt FX is going to go even lower, and NA can honestly really only go "up" from here - EMEA is relatively stable (only a 20 bps decline in margin), and APAC is, despite China, not down that much. India, Australia, SK, and Japan are showing pretty good trends overall, and the company's dependence on China seems to be waning, which is another good sign.

What's excellent as well is that operating expenses have remained stable. It tells me that despite all the negative trends here, the company has successfully managed to keep internal operational trends from getting worse.

Zeiss has failed to deliver guidance. In 2024/2025, the company expects the current China trends to be weaker in terms of sales of equipment. Things have not quite turned out as badly as expected for the company - the Chinese regulator has allowed limited resale for the stock of bifocal IOLs, which was the subject of a withdrawn tender, but the earnings risk is still at about €8M all in total. I now forecast a stiffer competition in pricing, and in China, as a result of the company's failure, the "buy local" policy is expanding.

At the very least, it makes sense to include this in estimates and cut forecasts for the company's growth.

However, that being said, I should remind you that AFX, or Zeiss, has never really been a massive growth company. Medtech companies, unless in a significant growth cycle, average 5-10% per year. Zeiss, even with current results, has averaged about 7-8% - completely normal.

The issue the company has been having is its premiumization. For well over 10 years, the company has traded above 20x P/E; for 8 years, even over 25-30x P/E. Its peak came in 2021, when the company traded at over 70x P/E. It's this normalization that needed to normalize - and normalize this trend has.

Too much or not yet enough?

Carl Zeiss Meditec - The valuation is too low

My stance is obviously that the valuation is too low. The current NAV, P/E or any multiple you choose to use does not in any way account for reversal, for market share, for expertise, or for the fact that the company is what I would consider the best in the world at what it does - and I'm not the only one to think this.

Of course, being the best doesn't mean that you're profitable. So we have to find a solid target for the company regardless. I want to point out, however, that even the company itself doesn't know where it's guidance is headed. That means that my current estimate is based more on guesswork than I'd like, but it's based on my previous target with my best estimate and knowledge for where this might go - while also considering that the market is very much overestimating the downside. To that fact, I argue that you can see when it dipped below 15x P/E, and the market reacted strongly. I bought a couple of shares (nothing major) below €25/share, which was my next-to-lowest PT alert I had on the stock, and that one is already up a few percent - but of course this doesn't even put a dent in my current negative RoR.

It's fair to say that Zeiss has been a disappointment. I expect a long recovery time. However, it's fair to say that I consider the fundamental risk to the company, insofar as it would go bankrupt, to be zero. There's no debt to speak of. One of the biggest risks I consider is actually the company, due to low valuation, becoming a target for a buyout or purchase. However, the company's capital structure makes the likelihood of that remote/impossible, because the entire business is majority owned by a trust, which is under statutory prohibition to put any of its shares or the company up for sale.

As such, we're in a situation where we just have to "wait" for the recovery - if that recovery comes.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of €85. I'm cutting this to €75/share as of this article. I maintain a high double-digit AEPS growth in 2027-2028 (as I mentioned in previous pieces) with a minimum AEPS level of about €2.4/share for the 2028-2029 period. With VISUMAX traction coupled with monetization of its backlog, shift to recurring revenue, cost control (as evidenced by results here), strong market position, and the implementation of DORC, I see potential for the company to rise to a €4-€5/share beyond 2030.

The current trend is a result of an unfortunate convergence of trends, many of which the company does not control. It does control its China focus and expansion, and I have made it clear that I am not a fan of this part of the company's strategy (and will continue to do so).

I considered for quite some time making the company a speculative investment, but I find and concur that I have no justifiable reason to do so. The way that I use speculative branding requires the company targeted to have fundamental issues related to its business operations or balance sheet. None of that is the case here.

I retain my approach, and I reiterate "STRONG BUY" for the company here.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk to Carl Zeiss remains, even in April 2026, a lengthened price/mix or market issue, as the company has been experiencing on and off for some time here. If this were to extend to longer periods, there would come a point where I would have to impact the company's fair value. As I've stated before, companies growing at much lower rates should not be valued at 30x+ P/E, especially not with a sub-2% yield. But that's not where we are; Carl Zeiss is instead a very strong grower of its revenues and earnings.

Secondary and tertiary risks (the ones that I would focus on here) are more related to tariffs, and we're seeing the impact of them here. Given the company's large amount of exposure to NA and the current tariff situation (even though at the time of writing, the existence of the tariffs is in question, given that it's a question for the Supreme Court in the USA), impacts here could be significant. Even with perhaps more clarity here, I believe this still poses a significant risk. Even with the tariffs as a risk, I view the company to be attractive well beyond its current price, with a significant upside potential.

Thesis

Carl Zeiss Meditec is a qualitative medtech business originating in Germany but with an international growth and sales profile. It does not offer a compelling yield, but what it does offer is a decently compelling overall long-term upside, even after the 2026 1Q that I am presenting here. Even conservatively speaking, if you accept some of the premiumization here, you have triple-digit upside potential, with 35% and potentially as high as 40% per year as of the end of the year 2027-2028, if you're willing to put the company above 20-25x P/E. For safety, I would probably limit myself to about 30x, but higher returns have been possible historically, and technically still are.

My fair value target is now, as of September 2025, a 30x-33x forward P/E, potentially and realistically upwards of 36x. The investment PT is now at €75/share, for a 15%+ annualized upside to this fair value target. I maintain my rating to "Strong Buy" as of this article. I have, however, lowered my share price target.

The translated PT for the company comes to an ADR target of $88/share for CZMWY.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 5/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of €75/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Strong Buy" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A New Era For The Fed? Looking Back On Kevin Warsh's U.S. Senate Hearing And Market Reactions
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Today welcomed one of the final steps for Kevin Warsh to replace Jerome Powell as Chairman for the Federal Reserve – a process that initially was supposed to occur on May 15th. However, the Trump Administration decided to spice things up with a Powell investigation that sent yet another wave of chaos in February.

But this is a relatively small detail, but one that would annoy the President even more, as the investigation would prevent Kevin Warsh's validation to pursue.

Highlights from Kevin Warsh's morning Senate hearing

The highly anticipated Senate confirmation hearing for incoming Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh took center stage this morning, and Wall Street is now frowning.

Stepping into the spotlight amid a backdrop of high geopolitical volatility, Warsh delivered a mixed address that instantly sent ripples across asset classes and triggered a decent market pullback.

At the core of his testimony was a bold declaration regarding monetary policy: Warsh explicitly stated his desire to reform the Federal Reserve, with notable calls for a review on forward guidance (that he wants to drop entirely) and a new inflation framework.

Rejecting the policy complacency of recent years (showing his disagreement for post-COVID policy), he signaled a structural shift in how the central bank will measure and react to price stability. Warsh's toughest point of view is on the Fed's balance sheet, which he wants to see reduced heavily over the coming years.

This would definitely not be as positive for stock markets. For markets that have grown accustomed to a highly accommodating Fed, this was a decent reality check. Wall Street really loves Jerome Powell, and his exit will surely be regretted by some.

Some tough questions, particularly from Senator Warren, on his swinging hawkishness, blasted the Fed Chair nominee – and he definitely dodged the answers.

Nevertheless, Warsh aggressively reinstated the narrative of strong Federal Reserve independence – but this one will have to be proven, as he never really answered questions about disagreeing with the President and other similar questions.

Add to the lingering uncertainty with the Middle East, and the market reaction got quite decisive. Equities took a decent hit as the reality of a more rigid Fed policy set in.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average led the intraday pullback, reflecting deep institutional caution as investors rapidly reassess the broader US economic outlook and a potential return to Middle East tensions.

With the critical April 22 US-Iran ceasefire deadline looming just hours away, Warsh's unyielding stance on inflation and institutional independence has thrown yet another puzzle for participants to play around with.

Vice President J.D. Vance has been reported to travel to Pakistan tomorrow morning (providing a de facto extension of the ceasefire if he really is departing).

What is sure is that the easy money era has officially been put on notice. Let's dive into the major movers of this busy, and quite risk-averse session. Financial markets are swinging more hawkish with the latest developments, but reactions have remained relatively restrained.

Energy Markets

WTI (US) oil prices have ground higher in recent hours but remain below the key $93 pivot.

The action in WTI remains stuck between $87 and $95 and should stay like that until participants learn more on the Iran issue.

Metals Markets

Metals are somehow quite offered in today's session, with drops of close to 3% across the board. Looking at reactions in the US dollar and other markets, it could just be profit-taking on the dip-buying at key technical levels, supplemented by some angst regarding the balance sheet reduction.

Gold found pressure at $4,900 which got exacerbated by the 4H 200-period MA.

US Dollar

The US dollar caught a decent bounce during the Kevin Warsh hearing. Hope that some of you explored our USD analysis on time to catch the first leg. Now retesting the 4H 50-period MA, the next move will be quite interesting (decent point for long entries on the USD, but watch out for volatile catalysts ahead).

Stock Markets

Stock markets are in the red today, but the reactions are, for now, still broadly contained. Be careful tomorrow, as things should be rocky, particularly if the second round of US-Iran talks fails to materialize.

Safe trades, and keep track of the evolution of the conflict ahead!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Workday: A Competitive Analysis With Oracle And SAP
data.description:
Investment thesis

Workday, Inc. (WDAY), is one of three leading competitors providing HCM (human capital management) and ERP (enterprise resource planning). Along with SAP SE (SAP) and Oracle Corporation (ORCL), it helps other firms do full-cycle human resource management and unify core business functions.

In this analysis, we compare the three companies across multiple financial metrics and conclude with a Buy rating for Workday. But, we also highlight the growth potential of one of the other two.

About Workday

The company offers enterprise cloud solutions powered by artificial intelligence [AI] to help companies "maintain accounting information; manage financial processes, such as payables and receivables; identify real-time financial, operational, and management insights; perform financial consolidation; reduce time-to-close; promote internal control and auditability; and achieve consistency across finance operations." (Source: Seeking Alpha).

SAP SE and Oracle Corporation provide similar but not identical offerings to their customers. For example, all three offer HCM and ERP services, but with different emphases.

The payroll consultancy firm Leppington Group offered this summary of the three firms:

"Oracle is ideal for large, global enterprises with complex payroll needs. SAP SuccessFactors is well-suited for organisations already using SAP ERP systems and requiring strong global capabilities. Workday is best for medium to large enterprises looking for a modern, user-friendly, and scalable solution."

And under the headline "Which Software Should You Buy in 2025", an article in AppCloneScript summarizes,

"Select Workday HCM if you want a holistic, intuitive, and contemporary HR software with inbuilt analytics and fast delivery. It's the preferred solution for customers in search of a proven technology foundation for the digital transformation of their business, as well as HCM professionals in organizations of any size who need to be part of a connected HCM experience.

"Go with SAP SuccessFactors if you're a large company with worldwide complexity, and especially if you're already invested in the SAP ecosystem. Its global payroll service and learning management are top of the market.

"Opt for Oracle HCM Cloud if you want a high level of configurability as well as robust analytics and integration with other Oracle apps. It applies to complicated business structures and hybrid clouds."

For investors who want exposure to HCM and/or ERP stocks, these three offer different approaches. Is one a better investment than the others? That is a question we will try to answer by analyzing their fundamentals.

Revenue, net income, and earnings

The following five-year tables show average year-over-year and forward growth rates for revenue and diluted EPS (from the growth tables at Seeking Alpha):

Workday's diluted EPS growth over the past five years is unavailable because it had a loss in 2023. That was followed by an unusually large gain in 2024.

What comes through is that Workday is a tech growth stock, while Oracle and SAP appear to be mature companies with only modest gains expected in coming years. That's reinforced by share repurchases and the payment of dividends by Oracle and SAP, while Workday continues to invest in growth (but does buy back shares). Investors interested in capital gains will be better off with Workday, while income investors should look at the other two companies (their dividend yields are comparable).

Margins and cash flow

When it comes to net margins, Oracle is clearly the leader, while Workday is the laggard, and SAP falls between them:

Surprisingly, the numbers are reversed for free cash flow per share, with Workday ahead of SAP and far ahead of Oracle:

Why has Oracle's FCF declined so much? A scan of its cash flow statement shows a huge increase in its net capital expenditures. It has gone from $6.87 billion in the fiscal year that ended in May 2024 to $21.22 billion in fiscal 2025 and now stands at $48.25 billion on a TTM basis.

At the same time, it is also loading up on long-term debt. That has risen from $76.26 billion in fiscal 2024 to $124.72 billion on a TTM basis. In both the capex and debt cases, it has invested heavily in AI research and development.

Comparing the three firms, Workday has an edge-for now, at least. Its FCF per share means it has more capital available for acquisitions and organic growth, leading to higher returns for shareholders. However, if Oracle's big investments pay off, it could become the undisputed leader in terms of margins and cash flow.

Returns on total capital

Return on total capital provides an overview of what shareholders are getting out of their capital contributions and the debt taken on by management:

Two contrasting scenarios have unfolded over the past several years. First, Workday and SAP are increasing their returns, and second, Oracle has been sliding down.

For Workday and SAP, artificial intelligence is driving new opportunities, as their customers seek to reduce costs, offset a shrinking workforce, and more. New technologies to achieve these challenges are emerging, and their customers want to take advantage of them. If they do not, they may suffer competitive disadvantages.

Oracle, too, has enjoyed growth for the same reason. And, as we saw, it has pushed up its capex and debt even more.

SAP is the best performer here, with Workday not far behind. Oracle is heading in the wrong direction-for now. Again, we point to Oracle's potential.

Valuations

Of course, investors will want to compare the valuations to determine how much it will cost them for one dollar of earnings. The following table provides two conventional measures and two PEG valuations, the latter to capture the value of earnings growth:

Note the very low PEG TTM ratio for SAP; that reflects the firm's rapid earnings growth in the past year. In Q4-2024, it earned $2.78 per share, and by Q4-2025, it had grown EPS to $7.22 per share, which is extraordinary growth. Since PEG FWD is more than 10 times higher, analysts expect that rate of growth to get back to normal going forward.

Workday's high PEG TTM ratio reflects the opposite of SAP's, with slow growth in the past year, from $1.98 to $2.61. However, its PEG FWD is much lower and better, with analysts expecting stronger growth this year.

All three stocks are better values now than they were in the second half of 2025, as their share prices slid, almost in unison:

EPS forecasts

Let's now look at the specific EPS estimates provided by Wall Street analysts (note the variations with the growth table figures above):

A couple of notes: first, the three firms have different year-ending dates, hence the Year 1, etc. Second, Workday is covered by 41 analysts, SAP by 8, and Oracle by 37. All three have fewer analysts as the forecasts go out further.

What do the results suggest? Earlier in this article, it appeared Workday was the growth stock to beat. Now, and with these data points, the growth lead may actually belong to Oracle. Its average annual EPS growth is expected to be roughly 50% higher than those of SAP and Workday, as its heavy capital and debt investments kick in and raise its fundamentals. Curiously, though, the analysts expect growth of only 7.00% in what I'm calling Year 2.

As we will see next, the companies have mostly beaten estimates in the past year.

Quality of earnings

Workday has consistently beaten estimates over the past four quarters. In the most recent quarter, analysts expected revenue of $2.52 billion, while the company delivered $2.53 billion. Similarly, on EPS, analysts forecast $2.32 per share, while the actual number was $2.47.

Oracle beat both revenue and earnings estimates in its latest quarter, Q3-fiscal-year-2026. In Q2, it beat on earnings and missed on revenue. Q1 saw double misses, while Q4-2025 produced beats on both revenue and EPS.

For SAP, Q4-2025 earnings topped estimates, while it missed on revenue. It was the same mix for Q3, Q2, and Q1.

Give Workday a slight lead in this category, with its consistent overperformance, a performance that should help maintain analysts' optimism about future results.

"Should" is an appropriate verb here. Of the 40 Workday revisions posted by Wall Street analysts, 10 are ups and 30 are downs. SAP, on the other hand, has six Ups and one down. Oracle has 34 ups and three downs. Analysts have far greater confidence in Oracle than in Workday and SAP, it seems.

Despite the revision numbers above, when it comes to ratings, Workday has 19 Strong Buys, seven Buys and 17 Holds.

For SAP, the analysts have nine Strong Buys, four Buys, and three Holds.

Oracle comes in with the best ratings: 28 Strong Buys, seven Buys, eight Holds, and one Strong Sell. Again, the edge goes to Oracle.

Turning to returns on total capital [ROTC], SAP has taken the lead from Oracle, while Workday lags them both:

Momentum

With the share price of all three firms declining since last October, there is no positive momentum.

Given that all fell about the same amount over about the same period, we should look to industry headwinds. That includes an increase in interest rates last fall, and increases in the cost of borrowing likely reduced demand among their customers.

The shareholder exit began about the same time as Oracle started boosting its capex and debt. Specifically, in the quarter that ended on November 30, 2025, Oracle boosted its capex by nearly 50%, from $8.50 billion to $12.03 billion. And in the fourth quarter, it upped its capex again by over 50%, from $12.03 billion to $18.64 billion. Investors in SAP and Workday likely asked themselves if their companies would need to make similar commitments.

And, there was other news that kept pressure on prices. In its Q4 fiscal 2026 first quarter earnings report, Workday reported subscription sales had slipped, albeit modestly.

Investors likely were concerned, too, by the increasing power of AI and what it might mean for HCM and ERP workflows. Would AI providers eat into existing business relationships? Could there be major disruptions? Would it drive down prices or margins?

The three firms are integrating AI into their processes and systems, but would that be enough to maintain their competitive edges?

Ratings

Despite these concerns, I believe the outlook for all three stocks is positive. They have well established positions in their industry, with most of big business as their customers. Incumbency is not a moat, but it is a solid advantage and provides time to adapt to the innovations of other firms.

As we saw in the EPS estimates, the analysts expect earnings to grow through at least the next three years. For Workday and SAP, we're looking at average expected returns of 14% to 15%, while Oracle is expected to post average returns of nearly 22%.

Looking specifically at Workday. I give it a Buy rating, based on its history of growing ROTC and beating estimates. And while investors may be concerned about HCM and ERP at the company, CEO Aneel Bhusri said on the Q4-2026 earnings call,

"You've all heard the narrative out there that HR and ERP will be replaced or relegated to the background by AI. I personally just don't see that happening. Our application domains are really, really hard to build. I've been working in the HR and ERP space for over 30 years. These are true systems of record that must process transactions with absolute accuracy and speed, enforce complex security models and comply with statutory and regulatory requirements all over the world. That kind of complexity is very hard to replicate. No amount of vibe coding is going to produce an HR or an ERP system."

Investors interested in Workday might also take a closer look at Oracle, which is expecting faster EPS growth because of its major capex and debt investments.

Interestingly, both Workday and Oracle are expected to keep growing their bottom lines, while their share prices show no sign of recovery. If this divergence continues to expand, there may be value investing opportunities in the near future.

Risk factors

The Seeking Alpha system has placed a warning label across the Workday summary page, warning the firm is at high risk of performing badly. It points specifically to decelerating momentum and negative EPS revisions. In my previous analyses of these warnings, I have found they depend too much on past and current data, and do not factor in forward potential. Nevertheless, investors should consider the warning, and investors considering Oracle and SAP should do the same.

The CEO of Workday considers the threat of AI disruption relatively low, but since we are in the early stages of AI development, investors should consider this threat. No one yet knows where the limits, or even the boundaries, lie.

In a highly competitive tech industry, it is essential to keep up with the leaders and even take the lead at least part of the time. All three companies face this challenge, but it may be more of a burden for Workday, which is smaller than its two rivals by market caps.

Securing customers data, which in the HCM and ERP areas is highly sensitive, is also essential. Any breach of data could lead to reputational and/or financial harm.

All three firms operate internationally, which brings in new exposures and risks. Compliance with regulations and legislation becomes more difficult with each new country they enter. From another perspective, foreign expansion also produces currency exchange risks.

Conclusion

I have confidence Workday will continue to deliver capital gains to its shareholders, along with modest gains through share buybacks. That is the main reason for my Buy rating. Based on the EPS estimates, I expect much the same from SAP, although shareholders in it will receive dividends as well.

Oracle, however, may have the greatest growth potential. The EPS estimates indicate strong capital gains as its investments show up in higher revenue and earnings.

data.title: Molina Healthcare: A Cyclical Long-Term Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Molina Healthcare (MOH) seems misunderstood after a temporary disruption in earnings, making now an attractive entry point for long-term investors. The severe Q4 underperformance appears to be driven by both Medicaid managed care's cyclical nature and retroactive rate adjustments, rather than fundamental problems in the business model.

In the past, Medicaid managed care has operated within a predictable cycle of cost increases that exceed state reimbursement rates. These ultimately return to normal after rates are adjusted. Therefore, it is likely that some form of adjustment in California rates will appear to help reduce some of the burden on Molina's profit margin. Marketplace prices have also increased since the start of 2026, and the Medicare Advantage Part D (MAPD) segment will be exited by 2027, further suggesting that the current downturn is temporary.

Lastly, the current outlook of $5.00 in EPS for 2026 does not seem to reflect normalized earnings potential. This includes ~$2.50 in one-time costs associated with ramping up in Florida and the exit of the Medicare Advantage business. If we were to remove these estimated costs, it is likely that Molina could earn ~$7.50 in normalized EPS. Furthermore, management has indicated that their "embedded earnings" model estimates the company's EPS could reach over $11 in the long term. This is based upon its growth pipeline of awarded government-backed contracts transitioning from investment phase to profitability in the next 12-24 months.

Given these developments, I believe current estimates aren't fully taking into account the recovery potential, contract maturation, or the removal of margin headwinds. This leads me to rate the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $188.60, where I expect this recovery to boost estimates as results materialize.

The Q4 Miss

The market's response to Q4 2025 was severe, with the stock dropping ~25% the day after reporting an adjusted EPS of -$2.75, down from $5.05 YoY. Molina noted that its Q4 results were weighed down by around $2.00 per share of unfavorable premium retroactive adjustments in its California Medicaid business. These are revenue items where the state retrospectively adjusted reimbursement, which is a common feature of Medicaid managed care that can often create earnings surprises. The Medicaid MCR for Q4 printed at 93.5%, elevated by cost pressure and timing mismatches, and the Medicare MCR hit 97.5%, driven by high-acuity utilization and the weak MAPD product. The Marketplace MCR was elevated at 92.5%.

With California retroactive adjustments reversing over time as rates catch up, it suggests that these pressures are temporary. The MAPD product, the single biggest negative Medicare item, is exiting entirely for 2027. Marketplace MCR is expected to normalize as rate increases averaging ~30% in 2026 work their way through the book. This is a common pattern in the Medicaid industry margin cycle, and with Molina's stock heavily impacted by these short-term headwinds, it creates an opportunity to buy at the trough as the industry normalizes.

Medicaid Rate Restoration

The managed care Medicaid space moves through a multiyear cycle of growth and decline. Each year the states adjust capitation rates based on predictions of what the medical services will cost. As long as the medical services provided are higher than the actuarially predicted costs (as was the case from 2024 to 2025), the managed care providers take the losses and lobby states for rate corrections. Once the state agrees to an increase (usually within 1-2 years after the initial loss), it allows the providers to make up for their losses by increasing their margins. This has happened multiple times with periods such as 2008-2010 (as unemployment rose), 2015-2017 (millions of new members due to the ACA), and currently 2024-2026. There's evidence that we are now at the trough, with Molina's CEO stating:

We believe our 2026 forecast for Medicaid is the trough for managed Medicaid margins. In this margin trough, we expect that Molina Medicaid will produce a low single-digit margin, not losses, and that the market is underfunded by 300 to 400 basis points.

Every time this has occurred, the managed care companies swung to profit and brought strong per-share gains with them within the next 12-24 months.

The $2.50 EPS Burden

The $5.00 in 2026 adjusted EPS guidance includes $2.50 in burdens explicitly stated by management. The first is a non-recurring $1.50 EPS burden related to the start-up costs associated with the new Florida CMS Medicaid contract. While it will take some time before the contract is profitable, it is estimated that the Florida contract will generate $6 billion in premium revenue yearly once operational. While it does pose short-term headwinds, it makes the long-term story much more rewarding now that those headwinds appear to have been priced in by the market.

The second element is a $1.00 EPS burden, which represents the underperformance of MAPD, which is being discontinued by 2027. At its current scale and composition of members, this product is structurally unprofitable. In 2027, this $1.00 EPS headwind will be $0, placing the company in a better position for long-term growth. When adding these two elements back to guidance, the normalized business is generating ~$7.50 in EPS for 2026. This estimate places them closer to past performance of $11.03 adjusted EPS in 2025, despite having a more unfavorable environment. With a more than 50% drop over the last year, the market appears to have overestimated the short-term headwinds given a likely return to strong earnings.

The $11+ Embedded Earnings

Management said that as a result of newly embedded earnings, the company now expects to earn more than $11 EPS in future years. This indicates the earnings power those contracts could contribute once they reach target margins. One contract alone, the Florida CMS contract, includes a $6 billion premium annually at Molina's historical Medicaid profit margin. These are not speculative contracts and have already been funded by governmental agencies. As the contracts mature over the following years, they move from the embedded earnings to tangible results. When combined with the restored rates on the rest of the current Medicaid book and removal of MAPD, the FY 2027 EPS comes into focus. With estimates of $8.20 EPS and a stock price at ~$150 per share, Molina is trading at about 18x FY 2027 earnings. This appears quite cheap for a managed care company at the beginning of a potential rate restoration cycle.

Upcoming Earnings

Molina reports its Q1 2026 earnings on April 22, 2026, with adjusted EPS estimates of $1.94. This number represents an expected decline from $6.08 YoY, which is already included in guidance. While Q1 has historically been the weakest season, Q1 2026 also absorbs the start-up costs associated with the Florida CMS contract and MAPD drag. Instead of looking at EPS, I'll be looking at other metrics to confirm rate progress.

The first is Medicaid MCR. The company's guidance calls for a rate of 92.9% in 2026, which is elevated over historical norms of 88-89%. A Q1 Medicaid MCR below 93.5%, combined with comments indicating cost trends are stabilizing or moderating, would represent the first signal that the trough is forming. On the other hand, a Q1 Medicaid MCR above 94% would be a negative indicator that the rate-cost imbalance has worsened prior to improving.

Any language regarding Medicaid rate restoration is also another thing to look out for. Specifically, management stated that the broad Medicaid market requires rates to exceed trend by 200-300 basis points to achieve program equilibrium. Any commentary on state rate filings, particularly California, Texas, and Ohio (Molina’s largest markets), would help determine how quickly the trough converts to recovery.

Finally, I will be watching the Florida contract ramp progress. Any update on member enrollment pace, operational build-out, or claims experience in Florida would help investors assess when the burden turns into contribution. Overall, positive developments on these fronts (which I expect) could help increase the potential for a re-rating of the stock's valuation as the market realizes the recovery is beginning.

Valuation

A price per share of ~$150 puts Molina at about 30x FY 2026 forecasted EPS. That seems a little pricey until we remove the temporary burden of $2.50 from our 2026 forecasted EPS. After removing this, the company would be trading at 20x normalized 2026 EPS. By FY 2027, the consensus estimate for EPS is $8.20, putting the stock at ~18x FY 2027 EPS currently. For my 12-month price target, I'm applying a 23x multiple, bringing the price per share to $188.60. This reflects a premium as Molina's story changes from a troubled provider to a diversified growth story, powered by interest rate normalization, the exit of MAPD losses, and the ramp-up of the Florida contracts.

Risks

The greatest risk is how long it will take for states to normalize Medicaid rates. If the major states are unable to do so in the coming years, then EPS would be negatively impacted and affect earnings.

Florida contract execution is the second risk. This is the largest contract in Molina's pipeline and represents $6 billion in annual premium with the state. Implementation costs of this contract also put an additional $1.50 EPS burden on the company. As a result, if the Florida startup slows down, guidance may miss expectations.

Conclusion

The response to Molina's earnings reset has caused the company to be valued based on short-term depressed profitability instead of its sustainable earnings potential. With better earnings visibility because of normalizing Medicaid rates, scaling in Florida, and the exit of Medicare Advantage losses by 2027, there will likely be greater clarity for earnings through 2027. This provides support for re-expansion of multiples, which is why I'm rating the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $188.60.

data.title: Retail Sales Jump 1.7% In March, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Advance Retail Sales Report from the Census Bureau showed consumer spending was more robust than expected in March. Headline sales jumped 1.7%, a sharp acceleration from February's 0.7% rise and above the projected 1.4% growth. This represents the largest monthly increase since January 2023.

The "Gas Price" Factor

While the 1.7% headline jump suggests a consumer boom, the data is heavily skewed by energy costs. Following the escalation of the war with Iran, gas prices surged 15.5% from the previous month, a record high. Because the retail report is not adjusted for price changes, a significant portion of this "growth" reflects the increased cost of filling a tank rather than increased consumer volume.

Here is the introduction from today's report:

Advance Estimates of U.S. Retail and Food ServicesAdvance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for March 2026, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $752.1 billion, up 1.7 percent (±0.4 percent) from the previous month, and up 4.0 percent (±0.5 percent) from March 2025. Total sales for the January 2026 through March 2026 period were up 3.7 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The January 2026 to February 2026 percent change was revised from up 0.6 percent (±0.4 percent) to up 0.7 percent (±0.2 percent).

Retail trade sales were up 1.9 percent (±0.5 percent) from February 2026, and up 4.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 10.1 percent (±1.8 percent) from last year, while food services and drinking places were up 2.4 percent (±1.9 percent) from March 2025.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 4.0% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales (Excluding Autos)

Core sales rose 1.9% in March, outperforming the expected 1.4%. However, because this metric still includes gasoline stations, it remains heavily influenced by the aforementioned energy price spike.

Core sales are up 5.5% year-over-year, the largest annual increase since early 2023.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

To find the true signal through the noise of the energy crisis, we look at "Control" purchases, which is an even more "core" view of retail sales. This series is the most reliable reading of the economy as it excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline stations, building materials, food services & drinking places.

Retail sales control purchases rose 0.8% in March. While this beat expectations of 0.2%, the gap between the 1.7% headline figure and the 0.8% control figure highlights how much the Iran-related gas spike inflated the primary numbers.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Current control purchases are up 4.8% compared to one year ago.

As shown in the chart below, the "control" series offers a much smoother, less volatile view of consumer health compared to the headline data, which is currently being whipped around by geopolitical instability and energy markets. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Macy's: Valuation Has Limited Upside With Technical And Macroeconomic Risks
data.description:
Two months after my previous analysis, we have seen how Macy’s, Inc. (M), hit its four-month low of $16.90. And despite the rebound attempts of the stock price, technical risks are still present. I can’t blame the market considering the increased macroeconomic risks that may weaken its performance further. Valuation is still quite cheap, while fundamentals are still robust. However, these don’t seem enough to justify upside.

Q4 2025: Challenges Persist, But It’s Surviving

As we all know, retailers are among the first ones to experience the direct impact of macroeconomic volatility amid stubborn inflation and tariff wars. Softer consumer spending and weaker purchasing power continue to hammer growth opportunities in this industry. Even an established name like Macy’s, Inc. could not avoid the situation despite having a strategic target market and pricing. Yet, its structural and operational efficiency allow it to stay afloat. This was evident in its most recent report.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $7.9B, down by 1.1% YoY from $8.0B. This reflected the mounting impact of stubborn inflation that continued to squeeze M’s pricing flexibility and consumer spending. This YoY decrease was also weaker than in my previous coverage, with a YoY decrease of only 0.2%. This happened despite the Fed’s efforts to boost economic activity and offset the impact of inflation.

On a lighter note, other revenue rose by 17% to $277M versus $239M YoY. Both components increased, which showed that the policy easing cycle was not totally futile. Credit card revenues net rose by $30M. M’s client base was also still solid as marketing and advertising-related revenues increased.

Meanwhile, COGS and SG&A expenses decreased, which can tell us that the decrease in revenue was also due to M’s decision to lower its capacity or volume. That way, it can avoid unnecessary expenses amid the rising prices. While they were not enough to completely offset the decrease in revenue, they helped M maintain positive profits. In the end, the operating margin remained decent at 7.1% despite decreasing from 7.9% YoY. Note that I did not include disposal, impairment, and interchange fees since they are exceptional expenses and not typical parts of the core business.

Risk Factors

As a known retailer, Macy’s is highly susceptible to risks associated with macroeconomic volatility. You can see how its net sales decreased despite its established reputation and wide domestic market coverage. Now, inflation is still above the 2.0% target, showing that the Fed is still having quite a hard time keeping it under control. After all, positive inflation means that prices are still increasing.

This may be fueled by the impact of the recent Middle East tension. Although the Iran and US ceasefire is still in effect, the skyrocketing oil prices are still evident. This will most likely raise fuel prices, which can increase M’s cost pressures, especially those related to its supply chain and transportation. More importantly, higher oil and fuel prices will raise the expenses in asset-heavy and large sectors like shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial. And because many industries depend on their services, the negative spillovers will extend to them. So, prices may increase further. This can squeeze the budget of many individuals and households, so M’s pricing power may also weaken. Growth opportunities may also thin.

Mitigants

Amid all these headwinds, I believe that M has some strengths and positive aspects to mitigate risks. Again, the phrase is "mitigate risks." I’d like to highlight that this section will highlight risk mitigants, not growth drivers. In the current market environment, I believe that M has limited sales and earnings growth drivers. But at least, I believe it will protect its operations.

One aspect to consider is its business model and pricing structure. Basically, M does not compete with cheaper retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN). Its premium products and pricing strategies mainly target middle- and upper-income households, not too rich but not too sensitive to inflation. With that, their spending is more resilient than the customers in the lower-income brackets. This may be the reason its sales decrease remains manageable. The only problem I see here is that customers may not increase their spending so as to please Macy’s. They may either keep their spending steady or reduce it a bit but not totally leave it. That’s just it, but at least its brand reputation ensures a loyal customer base.

Another is that the recent policy easing cycle may support spending using credit cards. This means lower borrowing costs for consumers. With that, using card payments may become crucial in ensuring liquidity among individuals and households. On top of that, M may receive credit card revenues from its bank partners and credit card issuers.

Lastly, robust liquidity ensures its longevity and sustainability. Looking at its balance sheet, M maintains adequate cash and cash equivalents of $1.2B, which is high enough to ensure enough reserves even if it incurs net losses or net cash outflows. Also, it has zero debt maturing within the year. The whole amount has a maturity of more than one year, and cash can cover half of the total. To be more specific, 99% of the total has a maturity of four years and longer. This is in line with the net debt/EBITDA of only 0.8x, which means that M only needs 0.8 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, we may prove that it has an ample buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess the valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model, or DDM. Its annualized dividend is now $0.77, so its dividend growth rate is also higher at 10.60%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 6.0% to be consistent with my previous coverage. It is also more conservative than the market standard of 5.0%. But note that the risk-free rate increased in line with the 10-year treasury yield. This has some downward effect on valuation. Lastly, Beta increased to 1.54, which means that M has become more volatile than in my previous coverage. With all this information, my new TP, or target price, is $25.08, which shows that M is cheap with some upside potential. This justifies the slight price rebound from the most recent low.

Meanwhile, its P/E of only 8.41x shows that M is only trading at that multiple relative to its EPS. However, note that EPS growth is not that explosive at only 5.84%. Using this, the PEG is 1.43x (8.41/5.87). This means that M is fully priced and trading more than its earnings growth.

Lastly, P/B using book value and P/CPS using cash per share show that M is still trading at a reasonable level and cheaper than the five-year average. My concern is that the gap between the TP and the current price is narrow. So, its price upside may not be explosive. Considering this, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

M’s technicals show rebound attempts, but bearish signals continue. The price trend is quite above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the current momentum is stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. This should not be surprising considering the pullback from December to March. However, the 50-day SMA is still decreasing, which suggests that recent market sentiments are still generally pessimistic. So, we still can’t guarantee that the rebound attempt will lead to a bullish breakout. We must note that the price highs and lows have been decreasing in the past three months, so the downtrend may hold.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI are still in line with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence yet. The MACD line is increasing above the signal line within positive territory along with the histogram, which implies that buying volume is increasing and outpacing signal volume. However, RSI is approaching the overbought region. And from the way I see it, it will fail to break above the first key resistance level of $22.78 before becoming overbought. With that, the dip and profit-taking that may follow may lead to a further low.

Conclusion

Macy’s, Inc. is experiencing the negative impact of macroeconomic volatility and geopolitical tensions. However, its solid and robust fundamentals assure us of its survivability and dividend longevity. Nonetheless, it is not enough to justify a rating upgrade. Valuation has limited upside, while technicals still show bearish tendencies despite rebound attempts. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Artisan Partners: Tech-Led Passive Managers May Benefit More From Energy Crisis Resolution
data.description:
Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. (APAM) has a decent amount of skew towards non-consensus strategies, meaning those weighted away from the largest gravity centers in value weighted indices namely the mega-caps and the US markets in general. With quite big AUMs in ex-US strategies and mid-cap, there is less inflection in the benchmark indices with respect to the outcome of the ceasefire and related peace talks around the Iran War, which is an important consideration for the asset management industry dragging on AUMs and a risk to performance. This is because the biggest effect of the Iran War happens to be on cost of capital assumptions, related to the reinflation concerns, as they have an outsized effect on high multiple stocks like the AI-driven megacaps, where lofty expectations backload a lot of value into discount rate-sensitive horizon values.
